back: A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and contains no partial dependencies, meaning no non-prime attribute is partially dependent on any candidate key.
A relation is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and contains no transitive dependencies, meaning no non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on any candidate key.
A partial dependency exists when a non-prime attribute is functionally dependent on part of a composite candidate key.
A transitive dependency occurs when a non-prime attribute depends on another non-prime attribute rather than directly on the candidate key.
A prime attribute is an attribute that is part of any candidate key of a relation.
A non-prime attribute is an attribute that is not part of any candidate key of a relation.
1NF requires that all attribute values are atomic (indivisible), there are no repeating groups, and each column contains values of a single type.
2NF eliminates partial dependencies that exist in 1NF relations with composite keys by ensuring non-prime attributes depend on the entire candidate key.
3NF eliminates transitive dependencies by ensuring non-prime attributes depend only on candidate keys, not on other non-prime attributes.
BCNF is a stricter version of 3NF where for every non-trivial functional dependency X → Y, X must be a superkey.
A lossless-join decomposition ensures that when relations are joined, no spurious tuples are generated; the original relation can be perfectly reconstructed.
Dependency preservation ensures that all functional dependencies can be enforced within the decomposed relations without requiring joins.
In R(A,B,C,D) with key {A,B} and FD A→C, attribute C is partially dependent on key {A,B} because C depends on A (part of the key).
In R(A,B,C) with FDs A→B and B→C, C is transitively dependent on A through B (which is non-prime).
